title: Using the standard library
instruction:
Java ships with thousands of ready-made classes in packages like `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`. Import them to use them:

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;  // specific class
import java.util.*;          // all of java.util
```

`java.lang` is auto-imported — String, Math, System are always available.
successMessage: java.lang is free — Math, String, System, Integer, etc. are always available. Everything else needs import. java.util.* is common in quick programs; explicit imports are preferred in production code.

title: Static imports
successMessage: sqrt and pow instead of Math.sqrt and Math.pow — cleaner for math-heavy code. Use static imports sparingly — they hide where methods come from.

title: java.util.Random
instruction: `Random` and `Math.random()` generate random numbers. `Random` is more flexible.
successMessage: Seeded Random gives reproducible results — great for tests. nextInt(6) gives 0-5, +1 shifts to 1-6. After 6000 rolls, each face appears approximately 1000 times — fair dice.

title: java.time — modern date/time
instruction:
Java 8 introduced `java.time` — the correct way to handle dates.

- `LocalDate` — date only
- `LocalTime` — time only  
- `LocalDateTime` — date + time
- `Duration` — time between two instants

Old `Date` and `Calendar` classes are deprecated and confusing — never use them.
successMessage: java.time is immutable, thread-safe, and intuitive. plusDays/minusDays for arithmetic. ChronoUnit.YEARS.between for age calculations. DateTimeFormatter for custom formats.

title: Useful java.util classes
instruction:
Quick tour of commonly used utilities:

- `Objects.requireNonNull()` — clean null checks
- `Objects.toString(obj, default)` — null-safe toString
- `UUID.randomUUID()` — unique IDs
- `Optional.of()` / `Optional.empty()` — null safety
successMessage: UUID is always 36 characters. Objects.requireNonNull is cleaner than if (x == null) throw. Objects.toString with default handles null safely. These are daily-use utilities.

title: Build a simple logger
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `Logger` class using `java.time` and `java.util.ArrayList`:
- Levels: `INFO`, `WARN`, `ERROR`
- `log(String level, String message)` — stores and prints `"[yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss] [LEVEL] message"`
- `getLogs(String level)` — returns all logs matching that level
- `getErrorCount()` — returns count of ERROR logs

Log at least 5 messages across different levels.
hint: Use LocalDateTime.now().format(formatter) for timestamps. Store logs as List<String>.
successMessage: A real logger with timestamped, leveled messages. This is exactly how SLF4J and Log4j work under the hood. Chapter 18 done!
